nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reef and rubble zones at 9–27 m depth; colonial burrower in mixed sand and rubble substrate, heavily influenced by cool California Current upwelling

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (minimum 20 cm of mixed fine sand and rubble)
broken coral rubble and shells for burrow construction

diet.notes: Primarily zooplanktivorous in the wild; has been documented accepting mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, prawn roe, and Calanus in captivity. Generally advised to feed small meaty portions two to three times daily, ideally via a pump discharge or flow that mimics drifting zooplankton.

avoidWith: other jawfish (unless mated pair or large tank with 90+ cm spacing per individual)

summary: A brilliantly blue-spotted cool-water jawfish from Mexico's Sea of Cortez, generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward tankmates but known to be territorial with conspecifics. Requires a deep sand-rubble substrate and a chilled, well-oxygenated aquarium.
careNotes:
The Blue Dot Jawfish is native to the Gulf of California, where cold upwelling from the California Current keeps water temperatures substantially cooler than typical tropical reef aquaria. Multiple sources indicate it generally does best in the 18–24°C range; Wikipedia specifically notes keeping it below 21°C to avoid stress and disease, while LiveAquaria lists 22–25.6°C — a significant disagreement. A dedicated chiller is commonly considered necessary for long-term success, and the temperature range used here (18–24°C) represents an average of available guidance; reviewers should verify against their specific livestock sourcing.

Adult size: most authoritative sources (FishBase, Wikipedia, Quality Marine) cite a maximum of ~10 cm; TFH Magazine and the Saltwater Aquarium Blog cite 4–6 inches (10–15 cm). The range used here (7.6–10.2 cm) averages the lower-end consensus and treats 10–10.2 cm as the shared upper bound. The higher TFH figure appears to be an outlier.

Escape risk is high — these fish are well-documented jumpers and will heave themselves out of an open tank. A tight-fitting canopy is not optional. They are burrowing species requiring a sand bed of at least 20 cm mixed with coral rubble, shells, and coarse material to construct and maintain their burrows. The fish seals its burrow at dusk and rebuilds the opening each morning.

They are colonial in the wild with individuals spaced roughly 1–3 meters apart. Multiple individuals can sometimes be kept together in a sufficiently large aquarium (generally advised to allow ~90 cm of horizontal spacing between burrow sites) or as a mated pair, but conspecific aggression is common without adequate space.

Breeding has been observed in captivity. The species is a paternal mouthbrooder; males display striking white anterior coloration during courtship in summer months and may dash several feet above their burrows to attract females. Eggs are brooded for approximately 5–7 days.

Avoid copper-based medications as a precaution (standard marine fish sensitivity); the species is reported to be hardy and resistant to parasitic infections compared to many reef fish, and is noted as an exceptional shipper.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder. Courtship and spawning have been reported in captivity, typically in warmer summer months. Males display by dashing 90–120 cm above their burrows and developing a stark white anterior coloration to attract females. Eggs are incubated in the male's mouth for approximately 5–7 days. Fry have been reported to reach adult size within roughly one year under aquarium conditions. Breeding difficulty in captivity is considered advanced due to the species' cool-water and deep-substrate requirements.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Designated secondary source from manifest. Provided native range, temperature (upper 50s–upper 70s °F; requires chiller), size (4–6 inches), substrate depth, diet, and breeding details. Temperature range is wider/cooler than LiveAquaria; size range extends higher than most other sources (possible outlier on upper end).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (family Opistognathidae, order Ovalentaria/misc — Wikipedia/Blenniiformes used as final authority), max size 10 cm TL, native range Eastern Central Pacific/Gulf of California, reef-associated habitat, diet of benthic and planktonic invertebrates.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Husbandry article from a professional marine wholesale supplier. Adult size up to 8.9 cm (3.5 in); temperature high 60s–mid 70s °F (~20–24°C); substrate minimum 10 cm depth (15–20 cm ideal); escape risk high; moderate-to-advanced care level; avoid triggers, groupers, lions, larger wrasses.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Cross-check for tank size (30 gal / 113.5 L confirmed), lifespan (3–5 years), diet (enriched brine shrimp, Formula 1, mysis, plankton), escape risk (high), breeding notes (spring/summer mouthbrooding), compatible tankmates list.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; courtship observed in summer months; males display white anterior coloration and vertical dashes above burrow; eggs brooded ~5–7 days; fry reported to reach adult size within one year.
